summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his college advice article with a self-deprecating preamble about the unreliability of career advice from older generations in computer science. He notes that college students wisely ignore their elders, whose predictions about technology careers tend to be wildly wrong. He then admits he's equally unqualified to give such advice but proceeds anyway.
key_points: Career advice from elders in computer science is often comically outdated and wrong
College students are better off ignoring most career predictions from previous generations
Self-awareness about one's ignorance doesn't necessarily prevent one from offering opinions anyway
money_quotes: Most college students, fortunately, are brash enough never to bother asking their elders for advice, which, in the field of computer science, is a good thing, because their elders are apt to say goofy, antediluvian things like "the demand for keypunch operators will exceed 100,000,000 by the year 2010."
I, too, have no idea what I'm talking about when I give advice to college students. But that's never stopped me from writing before.
key_insight: Technology career predictions age so poorly that the best qualification for giving advice may simply be the self-awareness to admit you're probably wrong.
